lesson.title: Electron Theory and Materials
lesson.subject: C&G 2365
lesson.tonePrompt: Teach like a strong electrical tutor: practical, direct, technically precise, concise, and natural.
keyIdeas: To establish the atomic foundation of electricity by explaining electron theory and how it dictates the properties of conductors and insulators.
The nucleus (protons and neutrons) and the orbiting electrons.
Electricity happens at the atomic level; you cannot understand current without understanding the particles that move.
Protons (positive), Electrons (negative), and Neutrons (neutral/no charge).
The attraction and repulsion between charges are the driving forces behind electron movement.
Valence electrons that are loosely bound and able to move from one atom to another.
The presence of free electrons is what makes a material useful for carrying electricity.
Conductors have many free electrons; insulators have tightly bound electrons.
Electricians must choose the right materials to either carry current (copper) or contain it (PVC).
The transition from random electron movement to a drift in a specific direction.

steps.title: Atomic Structure
steps.objective: Identify the three main parts of an atom and their locations.
keyIdeas: The nucleus is the stationary center of the atom.
Electrons are the orbiting particles that facilitate electrical activity.
The nucleus (protons and neutrons) and the orbiting electrons.
Electricity happens at the atomic level; you cannot understand current without understanding the particles that move.
Every material on a job site, from a copper busbar to a PVC conduit, is made of tiny building blocks called atoms.
At the very center of every atom is a heavy, fixed core called the nucleus.
anchorFacts: Every material on a job site, from a copper busbar to a PVC conduit, is made of tiny building blocks called atoms.
At the very center of every atom is a heavy, fixed core called the nucleus.
The nucleus contains two types of particles: protons and neutrons.
Tiny particles called electrons orbit around this central nucleus in layers known as shells.
In solid materials like wire, the nucleus stays in a fixed position while the electrons are the parts that can move.
Everything is made of atoms.
Atom is the name of the heavy, fixed core at the center of an atom.
misconceptionsToWatch: Thinking protons move during electrical flow; only electrons move in solid conductors.

Thinking that the entire atom moves during electrical flow; only the electrons move in solid conductors. | Think of a solar system where the nucleus is the sun and the electrons are the planets orbiting it. | Consider which part of the atom is described as 'fixed' and which part is 'orbiting'.

steps.title: Electrical Charge
steps.objective: Define the electrical charges associated with protons, neutrons, and electrons.
keyIdeas: Protons are positive (+).
Electrons are negative (-).
Neutrons have zero charge.
Opposites attract; likes repel.
Protons (positive), Electrons (negative), and Neutrons (neutral/no charge).
The attraction and repulsion between charges are the driving forces behind electron movement.
anchorFacts: Each particle in an atom carries a specific electrical charge that dictates how it interacts with others.
Protons carry a positive charge, which we represent with a plus (+) symbol.
Electrons carry a negative charge, represented by a minus (-) symbol.
Neutrons are neutral, meaning they have no electrical charge at all.
A key rule in electricity is that opposite charges attract each other, while like charges push each other away (repel).
Protons are positive (+).
misconceptionsToWatch: Believing neutrons have a 'negative' charge because they are 'neutral'.

Confusing 'neutral' with 'negative' because they both start with the letter N; remember N is for Neutral (zero). | Remember: P for Proton and Positive; N for Neutron and Neutral. | Think about what happens when you add a positive number to an equal negative number.

deeperAnswerGuidance: Positive and negative charges cancel out
The charges balance each other
The total charge becomes neutral
Equal amounts of opposite charges result in zero charge

steps.title: Free Electrons
steps.objective: Explain the concept of 'free electrons' in the outer shell of an atom.
keyIdeas: Only the outermost electrons are involved in electricity.
A 'free electron' is one that has broken away from its atom's valence shell.
Valence electrons that are loosely bound and able to move from one atom to another.
The presence of free electrons is what makes a material useful for carrying electricity.
Electrons orbit the nucleus in specific layers called shells.
The outermost layer is called the valence shell.
anchorFacts: Electrons orbit the nucleus in specific layers called shells.
The outermost layer is called the valence shell.
If an electron in the valence shell is far from the nucleus, the 'grip' or attraction holding it in place is weak.
These loosely held outer electrons can easily break away from their parent atom; we call these 'free electrons'.
The movement of these free electrons from one atom to the next is what creates an electrical effect.
Electrons live in shells.
misconceptionsToWatch: Assuming all electrons in an atom are free to move; only those in the outer shell typically participate in conduction.

Assuming all electrons in an atom are free to move; inner electrons are held too tightly to participate in conduction. | Think of free electrons as 'loose change' in a pocket that is easy to pull out and pass around. | Consider how the distance from the nucleus affects the 'grip' or attraction on the electron.

steps.title: Conductors vs Insulators
steps.objective: Distinguish between conductors and insulators based on their atomic structure.
keyIdeas: Conductors have many free electrons.
Insulators have very few or no free electrons.
The atomic 'grip' on electrons determines if a material is a conductor or insulator.
Conductors have many free electrons; insulators have tightly bound electrons.
Electricians must choose the right materials to either carry current (copper) or contain it (PVC).
A conductor is a material that has a large number of free electrons available to move.
anchorFacts: A conductor is a material that has a large number of free electrons available to move.
Metals like copper and aluminum are excellent conductors because their atomic structure allows electrons to drift easily between atoms.
An insulator is a material where the electrons are tightly bound to their atoms and cannot break free easily.
Materials like PVC (polyvinyl chloride) and rubber are insulators because they lack the free electrons needed to carry a flow.
Electricians use conductors to carry power and insulators to safely contain that power within the wire.
Conductors allow flow (many free electrons).
misconceptionsToWatch: Thinking insulators have no electrons at all; they have electrons, but they are not 'free' to move.

Believing insulators have no electrons at all; they have plenty of electrons, but they are 'trapped' and cannot move. | A conductor is like an open hallway; an insulator is like a locked room. | Think about the different jobs the inside and the outside of the cable must do.

PVC is an insulator to contain current
Copper has many free electrons for flow
PVC prevents the flow from escaping the wire
It ensures safety by blocking electron movement to the outside
steps.hint: A conductor is like an open hallway; an insulator is like a locked room.

steps.title: Electron Flow and Current
steps.objective: Define electric current as the directed flow of electrons through a material.
keyIdeas: Current is the organized movement of electrons in one direction.
Random movement does not result in a measurable current.
The transition from random electron movement to a drift in a specific direction.
This bridges the gap between abstract atomic theory and practical electrical current.
In a piece of copper wire sitting on a bench, free electrons move randomly in all directions.
When an electrical force is applied, these free electrons stop moving randomly and begin to drift in a single, unified direction.
anchorFacts: In a piece of copper wire sitting on a bench, free electrons move randomly in all directions.
When an electrical force is applied, these free electrons stop moving randomly and begin to drift in a single, unified direction.
This directed, continuous flow of free electrons through a conductor is called an electric current.
The more electrons that pass a specific point in the circuit every second, the higher the current flow.
Current only flows effectively when there is a continuous path of conductive material for the electrons to follow.
Random movement is not current.
misconceptionsToWatch: Confusing the speed of individual electrons with the speed of the electrical signal.

Confusing the speed of the electrical signal (which is near the speed of light) with the physical speed of the electrons (which is actually quite slow). | Think of a crowd of people in a hallway; current is when they all start marching in the same direction together. | Compare 'random movement' with 'directed flow'.

steps.title: Worked Example: Identifying Conductors and Insulators
steps.objective: Demonstrate how to identify a material's electrical property by examining its atomic 'willingness' to share electrons.
keyIdeas: Demonstrate how to identify a material's electrical property by examining its atomic 'willingness' to share electrons.
Show one worked example.

Scenario: You are stripping a standard 2.5mm² Twin and Earth cable. You have the bare copper wire in one hand and the grey PVC outer sheath in the other. You need to explain why they behave differently using electron theory.

Step 1: Examine the copper core. At an atomic level, copper has a valence shell (outer layer) where the electrons are far from the nucleus. This weak attraction means there are millions of 'free electrons' ready to move from atom to atom.

Step 2: Examine the PVC sheath. In this material, the atoms hold onto their electrons very tightly. There are almost no 'free electrons' available to break away, meaning the electrons are 'trapped' in their orbits.
Step 3: Apply the definitions. Because the copper has a high density of free electrons, it is classified as a conductor. Because the PVC lacks free electrons, it is classified as an insulator.
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are stripping a standard 2.5mm² Twin and Earth cable. You have the bare copper wire in one hand and the grey PVC outer sheath in the other. You need to explain why they behave differently using electron theory.

Step 1: Examine the copper core. At an atomic level, copper has a valence shell (outer layer) where the electrons are far from the nucleus. This weak attraction means there are millions of 'free electrons' ready to move from atom to atom.

Step 2: Examine the PVC sheath. In this material, the atoms hold onto their electrons very tightly. There are almost no 'free electrons' available to break away, meaning the electrons are 'trapped' in their orbits.
Step 3: Apply the definitions. Because the copper has a high density of free electrons, it is classified as a conductor. Because the PVC lacks free electrons, it is classified as an insulator.
Final Result: The copper core is the conductor used to carry the electric current, while the PVC is the insulator used to safely contain that current within the wire.

{
  "lessonAim": "To establish the atomic foundation of electricity by explaining electron theory and how it dictates the properties of conductors and insulators.",
  "taskMode": "respond",
  "teachCheckCount": 5,
  "teachChecks": [
    {
      "key": "atomic-structure",
      "title": "Atomic Structure",
      "objective": "Identify the three main parts of an atom and their locations.",
      "conceptFocus": "The nucleus (protons and neutrons) and the orbiting electrons.",
      "whyItMatters": "Electricity happens at the atomic level; you cannot understand current without understanding the particles that move.",
      "misconceptions": [
        "Thinking protons move during electrical flow; only electrons move in solid conductors."
      ]
    },
    {
      "key": "electrical-charge",
      "title": "Electrical Charge",
      "objective": "Define the electrical charges associated with protons, neutrons, and electrons.",
      "conceptFocus": "Protons (positive), Electrons (negative), and Neutrons (neutral/no charge).",
      "whyItMatters": "The attraction and repulsion between charges are the driving forces behind electron movement.",
      "misconceptions": [
        "Believing neutrons have a 'negative' charge because they are 'neutral'."
      ]
    },
    {
      "key": "free-electrons",
      "title": "Free Electrons",
      "objective": "Explain the concept of 'free electrons' in the outer shell of an atom.",
      "conceptFocus": "Valence electrons that are loosely bound and able to move from one atom to another.",
      "whyItMatters": "The presence of free electrons is what makes a material useful for carrying electricity.",
      "misconceptions": [
        "Assuming all electrons in an atom are free to move; only those in the outer shell typically participate in conduction."
      ]
    },
    {
      "key": "conductors-insulators",
      "title": "Conductors vs Insulators",
      "objective": "Distinguish between conductors and insulators based on their atomic structure.",
      "conceptFocus": "Conductors have many free electrons; insulators have tightly bound electrons.",
      "whyItMatters": "Electricians must choose the right materials to either carry current (copper) or contain it (PVC).",
      "misconceptions": [
        "Thinking insulators have no electrons at all; they have electrons, but they are not 'free' to move."
      ]
    },
    {
      "key": "electron-flow-current",
      "title": "Electron Flow and Current",
      "objective": "Define electric current as the directed flow of electrons through a material.",
      "conceptFocus": "The transition from random electron movement to a drift in a specific direction.",
      "whyItMatters": "This bridges the gap between abstract atomic theory and practical electrical current.",
      "misconceptions": [
        "Confusing the speed of individual electrons with the speed of the electrical signal."
      ]
    }
  ],
  "workedExampleObjective": "Demonstrate how to identify a material's electrical property by examining its atomic 'willingness' to share electrons.",
  "guidedPracticeObjective": "Classify a list of common site materials (Copper, PVC, Gold, Rubber, Steel) as either conductors or insulators based on electron theory.",
  "practiceObjective": "Verify the learner can correctly identify atomic particles, their charges, and the fundamental difference between conductive and insulating materials.",
  "integrativeObjective": "Synthesize electron theory with material selection to explain why a standard 2.5mm² cable uses both a copper core and a thermoplastic sheath.",
  "spacedReviewObjective": "Recall the charges of atomic particles and the definition of a free electron from previous theory.",
  "inScope": [
    "Protons, neutrons, and electrons",
    "Atomic nucleus and electron shells",
    "Positive, negative, and neutral charges",
    "Free electrons and valence shells",
    "Definition of a conductor",
    "Definition of an insulator",
    "Examples of conductive and insulating materials"
  ],
  "outOfScope": [
    "Ohm's Law calculations",
    "Resistivity formula (rho)",
    "Voltage drop calculations",
    "Power (Watts) and energy",
    "Chemical effects of cells/batteries",
    "Thermal effects of current"
  ],
  "constraints": [
    "Strictly follow the 'teach-before-test' principle.",
    "Do not use terms like 'Resistance' or 'Voltage' as technical definitions until they are introduced in later lessons.",

Critical rules:
- Terms must be lesson-specific and grounded in the source.
- Definitions must be short and technically defensible.
- anchorPhrases should be reusable phrases that later phases can teach verbatim or near-verbatim.

terms.simpleDefinition: The basic building block of all matter, consisting of a central nucleus and orbiting electrons.
terms.anchor: Think of it like a tiny solar system with a sun in the middle and planets orbiting around.
terms.misconception: Thinking atoms are solid balls; they are actually mostly empty space with particles inside.

terms.simpleDefinition: The heavy central part of an atom containing protons and neutrons.
terms.anchor: The 'engine room' at the very center of the atom.
terms.misconception: Believing the nucleus moves during electrical flow; it stays fixed while electrons move.

terms.simpleDefinition: A particle inside the nucleus that carries a positive (+) electrical charge.
terms.anchor: P for Proton, P for Positive.
terms.misconception: Thinking protons flow through a copper wire; only electrons move in solid conductors.

terms.simpleDefinition: A particle inside the nucleus that has no electrical charge (it is neutral).
terms.anchor: N for Neutron, N for Neutral.
terms.misconception: Confusing 'neutral' with 'negative' because they both start with N.

terms.simpleDefinition: A tiny particle orbiting the nucleus that carries a negative (-) electrical charge.
terms.anchor: The 'planets' orbiting the nucleus that do all the work in electricity.
terms.misconception: Thinking electrons are the same size as protons; they are actually much, much smaller.

terms.simpleDefinition: An electron in the outermost shell of an atom that can easily break away and move to another atom.
terms.anchor: The 'loose change' in an atom's pocket that is easy to spend.
terms.misconception: Assuming all electrons in an atom are free to move; only the ones furthest from the nucleus usually move.

terms.simpleDefinition: A material, like copper or aluminum, that has many free electrons and allows electricity to flow easily.
terms.misconception: Thinking a conductor 'creates' electricity; it simply provides the path for electrons to move.

terms.simpleDefinition: A material, like PVC or rubber, where electrons are tightly bound and cannot move easily.
terms.anchor: A brick wall that stops electrons from passing through.
terms.misconception: Believing insulators have no electrons at all; they have plenty, but they aren't 'free' to move.

terms.simpleDefinition: The directed flow of free electrons through a conductor.
terms.anchor: A marching army of electrons all moving in the same direction.
terms.misconception: Confusing the speed of the electrical signal with the physical speed of the individual electrons.

anchorPhrases: Opposite charges attract; like charges repel.
Electricity is the movement of free electrons.
Conductors have many free electrons; insulators have very few.
The nucleus stays put; the electrons do the traveling.
misconceptionTargets: Protons do not move through wires; only electrons move.
Insulators still contain atoms and electrons, they just aren't 'free' ones.
Neutrons are neutral (zero charge), not negative.
A material is a conductor because of its atomic structure, not just because it is a metal.

  "terms": [

      "term": "Atom",
      "simpleDefinition": "The basic building block of all matter, consisting of a central nucleus and orbiting electrons.",
      "anchor": "Think of it like a tiny solar system with a sun in the middle and planets orbiting around.",
      "misconception": "Thinking atoms are solid balls; they are actually mostly empty space with particles inside."
    },
    {
      "term": "Nucleus",
      "simpleDefinition": "The heavy central part of an atom containing protons and neutrons.",
      "anchor": "The 'engine room' at the very center of the atom.",
      "misconception": "Believing the nucleus moves during electrical flow; it stays fixed while electrons move."
    },
    {
      "term": "Proton",
      "simpleDefinition": "A particle inside the nucleus that carries a positive (+) electrical charge.",
      "anchor": "P for Proton, P for Positive.",
      "misconception": "Thinking protons flow through a copper wire; only electrons move in solid conductors."
    },
    {
      "term": "Neutron",
      "simpleDefinition": "A particle inside the nucleus that has no electrical charge (it is neutral).",
      "anchor": "N for Neutron, N for Neutral.",
      "misconception": "Confusing 'neutral' with 'negative' because they both start with N."
    },
    {
      "term": "Electron",
      "simpleDefinition": "A tiny particle orbiting the nucleus that carries a negative (-) electrical charge.",
      "anchor": "The 'planets' orbiting the nucleus that do all the work in electricity.",
      "misconception": "Thinking electrons are the same size as protons; they are actually much, much smaller."
    },
    {
      "term": "Free Electron",
      "simpleDefinition": "An electron in the outermost shell of an atom that can easily break away and move to another atom.",
      "anchor": "The 'loose change' in an atom's pocket that is easy to spend.",
      "misconception": "Assuming all electrons in an atom are free to move; only the ones furthest from the nucleus usually move."
    },
    {
      "term": "Conductor",
      "simpleDefinition": "A material, like copper or aluminum, that has many free electrons and allows electricity to flow easily.",
      "anchor": "A wide-open motorway for electrons.",
      "misconception": "Thinking a conductor 'creates' electricity; it simply provides the path for electrons to move."
    },
    {
      "term": "Insulator",
      "simpleDefinition": "A material, like PVC or rubber, where electrons are tightly bound and cannot move easily.",
      "anchor": "A brick wall that stops electrons from passing through.",
      "misconception": "Believing insulators have no electrons at all; they have plenty, but they aren't 'free' to move."
    },
    {
      "term": "Electric Current",
      "simpleDefinition": "The directed flow of free electrons through a conductor.",
      "anchor": "A marching army of electrons all moving in the same direction.",
      "misconception": "Confusing the speed of the electrical signal with the physical speed of the individual electrons."
    }
  ],
  "anchorPhrases": [
    "Opposite charges attract; like charges repel.",
    "Electricity is the movement of free electrons.",
    "Conductors have many free electrons; insulators have very few.",

  ],

    "Neutrons are neutral (zero charge), not negative.",
    "A material is a conductor because of its atomic structure, not just because it is a metal."

}

teachChecks.title: Atomic Structure
teachChecks.objective: Identify the three main parts of an atom and their locations.
teachingPoints: Every material on a job site, from a copper busbar to a PVC conduit, is made of tiny building blocks called atoms.
At the very center of every atom is a heavy, fixed core called the nucleus.
The nucleus contains two types of particles: protons and neutrons.
Tiny particles called electrons orbit around this central nucleus in layers known as shells.
In solid materials like wire, the nucleus stays in a fixed position while the electrons are the parts that can move.
keyIdeas: The nucleus is the stationary center of the atom.
Electrons are the orbiting particles that facilitate electrical activity.
misconceptions: Thinking that the entire atom moves during electrical flow; only the electrons move in solid conductors.
retrievalTextLines: Everything is made of atoms.
The nucleus is the center.
Protons and neutrons live in the nucleus.
Electrons orbit the outside.
teachChecks.hint: Think of a solar system where the nucleus is the sun and the electrons are the planets orbiting it.

teachChecks.title: Electrical Charge
teachChecks.objective: Define the electrical charges associated with protons, neutrons, and electrons.
teachingPoints: Each particle in an atom carries a specific electrical charge that dictates how it interacts with others.
Protons carry a positive charge, which we represent with a plus (+) symbol.
Electrons carry a negative charge, represented by a minus (-) symbol.
Neutrons are neutral, meaning they have no electrical charge at all.
A key rule in electricity is that opposite charges attract each other, while like charges push each other away (repel).
keyIdeas: Protons are positive (+).
Electrons are negative (-).
Neutrons have zero charge.
Opposites attract; likes repel.
misconceptions: Confusing 'neutral' with 'negative' because they both start with the letter N; remember N is for Neutral (zero).
retrievalTextLines: Protons are positive (+).
Electrons are negative (-).
Neutrons are neutral (0).
Opposites attract.
teachChecks.hint: Remember: P for Proton and Positive; N for Neutron and Neutral.

teachChecks.title: Free Electrons
teachChecks.objective: Explain the concept of 'free electrons' in the outer shell of an atom.
teachingPoints: Electrons orbit the nucleus in specific layers called shells.
The outermost layer is called the valence shell.
If an electron in the valence shell is far from the nucleus, the 'grip' or attraction holding it in place is weak.
These loosely held outer electrons can easily break away from their parent atom; we call these 'free electrons'.
The movement of these free electrons from one atom to the next is what creates an electrical effect.
keyIdeas: Only the outermost electrons are involved in electricity.
A 'free electron' is one that has broken away from its atom's valence shell.
misconceptions: Assuming all electrons in an atom are free to move; inner electrons are held too tightly to participate in conduction.
retrievalTextLines: Electrons live in shells.
The outer shell is the valence shell.
Loosely held electrons can break away.
These are called free electrons.
teachChecks.hint: Think of free electrons as 'loose change' in a pocket that is easy to pull out and pass around.

teachChecks.title: Conductors vs Insulators
teachChecks.objective: Distinguish between conductors and insulators based on their atomic structure.
teachingPoints: A conductor is a material that has a large number of free electrons available to move.
Metals like copper and aluminum are excellent conductors because their atomic structure allows electrons to drift easily between atoms.
An insulator is a material where the electrons are tightly bound to their atoms and cannot break free easily.
Materials like PVC (polyvinyl chloride) and rubber are insulators because they lack the free electrons needed to carry a flow.
Electricians use conductors to carry power and insulators to safely contain that power within the wire.
keyIdeas: Conductors have many free electrons.
Insulators have very few or no free electrons.
The atomic 'grip' on electrons determines if a material is a conductor or insulator.
misconceptions: Believing insulators have no electrons at all; they have plenty of electrons, but they are 'trapped' and cannot move.
retrievalTextLines: Conductors allow flow (many free electrons).
Insulators block flow (tightly bound electrons).
Copper is a conductor.
PVC is an insulator.
teachChecks.hint: A conductor is like an open hallway; an insulator is like a locked room.

teachChecks.title: Electron Flow and Current
teachChecks.objective: Define electric current as the directed flow of electrons through a material.
teachingPoints: In a piece of copper wire sitting on a bench, free electrons move randomly in all directions.
When an electrical force is applied, these free electrons stop moving randomly and begin to drift in a single, unified direction.
This directed, continuous flow of free electrons through a conductor is called an electric current.
The more electrons that pass a specific point in the circuit every second, the higher the current flow.
Current only flows effectively when there is a continuous path of conductive material for the electrons to follow.
keyIdeas: Current is the organized movement of electrons in one direction.
Random movement does not result in a measurable current.
misconceptions: Confusing the speed of the electrical signal (which is near the speed of light) with the physical speed of the electrons (which is actually quite slow).
retrievalTextLines: Random movement is not current.
Directed movement is current.
Current is the flow of free electrons.
teachChecks.hint: Think of a crowd of people in a hallway; current is when they all start marching in the same direction together.

    {
      "term": "Neutron",
      "simpleDefinition": "A particle inside the nucleus that has no electrical charge (it is neutral).",
      "anchor": "N for Neutron, N for Neutral.",
      "misconception": "Confusing 'neutral' with 'negative' because they both start with N."
    },
    {
      "term": "Electron",
      "simpleDefinition": "A tiny particle orbiting the nucleus that carries a negative (-) electrical charge.",
      "anchor": "The 'planets' orbiting the nucleus that do all the work in electricity.",
      "misconception": "Thinking electrons are the same size as protons; they are actually much, much smaller."
    },
    {
      "term": "Free Electron",
      "simpleDefinition": "An electron in the outermost shell of an atom that can easily break away and move to another atom.",
      "anchor": "The 'loose change' in an atom's pocket that is easy to spend.",
      "misconception": "Assuming all electrons in an atom are free to move; only the ones furthest from the nucleus usually move."
    },
    {
      "term": "Conductor",
      "simpleDefinition": "A material, like copper or aluminum, that has many free electrons and allows electricity to flow easily.",
      "anchor": "A wide-open motorway for electrons.",
      "misconception": "Thinking a conductor 'creates' electricity; it simply provides the path for electrons to move."
    },
    {
      "term": "Insulator",
      "simpleDefinition": "A material, like PVC or rubber, where electrons are tightly bound and cannot move easily.",
      "anchor": "A brick wall that stops electrons from passing through.",
      "misconception": "Believing insulators have no electrons at all; they have plenty, but they aren't 'free' to move."
    },
    {
      "term": "Electric Current",
      "simpleDefinition": "The directed flow of free electrons through a conductor.",
      "anchor": "A marching army of electrons all moving in the same direction.",
      "misconception": "Confusing the speed of the electrical signal with the physical speed of the individual electrons."
    }
  ],
  "anchorPhrases": [
    "Opposite charges attract; like charges repel.",
    "Electricity is the movement of free electrons.",
    "Conductors have many free electrons; insulators have very few.",
    "The nucleus stays put; the electrons do the traveling."
  ],
  "misconceptionTargets": [
    "Protons do not move through wires; only electrons move.",
    "Insulators still contain atoms and electrons, they just aren't 'free' ones.",
    "Neutrons are neutral (zero charge), not negative.",
    "A material is a conductor because of its atomic structure, not just because it is a metal."
  ]
}

Critical rules:
- basicQuestions must contain exactly 3 items.
- Each basic question must test one taught fact or one taught distinction only.
- Each basic question must be traceable to the teachingPoints from Phase 3.
- sourceTeachingPointIds must point to the teaching point indexes that justify the question.
- Each basic question answerGuidance must contain 2-5 checkable phrases or ideas.
- Never ask the learner to supply multiple separate facts in one basic question.
- Prefer single-fact or single-term checks that support precise corrective feedback.
- If a question asks for the specific term, name, abbreviation expansion, code letter, or technical label, the first answerGuidance item must be the exact target from the lesson vocabulary.
- If a question defines something and asks for its name or type, answerGuidance must contain the exact technical term that matches that definition.
- For exact-term questions, answerGuidance may include only tight equivalents of that same target. Do not accept loose descriptive paraphrases, topic labels, or nearby concepts from other questions.
- If a question asks what one code letter stands for, answerGuidance must contain only that code-letter expansion word or a tight grammatical variant, not a component name or neighbouring concept.
- Keep each answerGuidance list local to its own question only. Do not copy valid answers from adjacent questions in the same step.
- ALWAYS keep each basic question narrow, exact, and easy to mark from one response.
- ALWAYS keep exact-term answers exact. Repeat the canonical target, not a broader topic label.
- ALWAYS prefer one precise answer target over a wider but weaker guidance list.
- The deeper question must connect named teaching points and ask why/how/what-must-be-true.
- deeperAnswerGuidance must contain 3-6 checkable ideas.
- deeperSourceTeachingPointIds must identify the teaching points that justify the deeper question.

Example of a good exact-term check:
{"questionText":"What does PV stand for?","answerGuidance":["Photovoltaic"]}
Bad version:
{"questionText":"What does PV stand for?","answerGuidance":["Photovoltaic","PV","Photovoltaic generation"]}
Example of a good code-letter check:
{"questionText":"In TN-S, what does the S stand for?","answerGuidance":["Separate"]}
Bad version:
{"questionText":"In TN-S, what does the S stand for?","answerGuidance":["Separate","Earthing conductor","TN-S system"]}
Example of a good unit check:
{"questionText":"What is the unit of resistance?","answerGuidance":["Ohm","Ohms","Ω"]}
Bad version:
{"questionText":"What is the unit of resistance?","answerGuidance":["Resistance","Meaning of resistance"]}

Completion requirement:
- Always keep each check narrow, exact, and local to one question only.
- Always keep exact-term answers exact.

teachChecks.title: Atomic Structure

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the name of the heavy, fixed core at the center of an atom?
basicQuestions.hint: It is the central part that contains protons and neutrons.

basicQuestions.questionText: Which particle orbits around the central nucleus of an atom?
basicQuestions.hint: These are the tiny particles that move in layers or shells.

basicQuestions.questionText: What are the layers called in which electrons orbit the nucleus?
basicQuestions.hint: Think of the layers of an onion or the orbits of planets.

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: In a solid copper wire, why is it only the electrons that move to create electricity rather than the nucleus?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The nucleus is a fixed core
The nucleus is stationary
Electrons are the orbiting particles
Electrons are the parts that can move
teachChecks.hint: Consider which part of the atom is described as 'fixed' and which part is 'orbiting'.

teachChecks.title: Electrical Charge

basicQuestions.questionText: What type of electrical charge does a proton carry?
basicQuestions.hint: Remember: P for Proton and P for...

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the electrical charge of a neutron?
basicQuestions.hint: Remember: N for Neutron and N for...

basicQuestions.questionText: What do two electrical charges of the same type (like charges) do when placed near each other?
basicQuestions.hint: Opposites attract, but identical charges do the opposite.

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: If an atom contains an equal number of protons and electrons, why does it have no overall electrical charge?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Positive and negative charges cancel out
The charges balance each other
The total charge becomes neutral
Equal amounts of opposite charges result in zero charge
teachChecks.hint: Think about what happens when you add a positive number to an equal negative number.

teachChecks.title: Free Electrons

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the specific name for the outermost shell of an atom?
basicQuestions.hint: This is the layer furthest from the nucleus.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the term for an electron that has broken away from its parent atom?
basicQuestions.hint: These are the loosely held electrons that facilitate electrical flow.

basicQuestions.questionText: In which shell of an atom are the electrons that are most likely to participate in electrical conduction located?
basicQuestions.hint: It is the shell where the attraction from the nucleus is weakest.

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why are electrons in the valence shell more likely to become 'free' than those in the shells closer to the nucleus?
deeperAnswerGuidance: They are further from the nucleus
The attraction holding them is weak
The grip of the nucleus is weaker at a distance
They can easily break away
teachChecks.hint: Consider how the distance from the nucleus affects the 'grip' or attraction on the electron.

teachChecks.title: Conductors vs Insulators

basicQuestions.questionText: What does a conductor have a large number of that allows electricity to flow?
basicQuestions.hint: These are the loosely bound particles in the valence shell.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the name for a material where electrons are tightly bound and cannot move easily?
basicQuestions.hint: Think of materials like PVC or rubber.

basicQuestions.questionText: Which common insulating material is used to contain the flow of electricity in a standard cable?
basicQuestions.hint: It is the plastic-like coating on the outside of the wire.

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why is a cable constructed with a copper core surrounded by a PVC sheath rather than being made entirely of one material?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Copper is a conductor to carry current
PVC is an insulator to contain current
Copper has many free electrons for flow
PVC prevents the flow from escaping the wire
It ensures safety by blocking electron movement to the outside
teachChecks.hint: Think about the different jobs the inside and the outside of the cable must do.

teachChecks.title: Electron Flow and Current

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the term for the directed, continuous flow of free electrons through a conductor?
basicQuestions.hint: This is the organized movement of electrons in one direction.

basicQuestions.questionText: What word describes the unified movement of electrons in a single direction when a force is applied?
basicQuestions.hint: It is the opposite of random movement.

basicQuestions.questionText: What must be provided for electrons to flow effectively through a circuit?
basicQuestions.hint: Electrons cannot flow if there is a break in the material.

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: How does the movement of free electrons in a copper wire change when it is connected to a power source compared to when it is disconnected?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Changes from random movement to directed flow
Electrons begin to drift in one direction
Movement becomes unified instead of random
An electric current is formed

Unit: 202 - Principles of Electrical Science

Learning outcome: LO4 - Resistance, resistivity, voltage, current, power

Lesson: 202-4A - Electron Theory and Materials

Topic focus: Electron Theory and Materials

Prerequisites: 202-3B

Must-have topics: Basic electron theory; Conductors vs insulators

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 202.LO4.AC4.1: Basic electron theory
- 202.LO4.AC4.2: Conductors vs insulators

Lesson split rationale: Establishes the atomic basis of electricity and how it determines the properties of conductors and insulators.

In-scope teaching anchors: Basic electron theory; Conductors vs insulators

Out-of-scope topics: Resistance, Resistivity, and Voltage Drop; DC Circuit Relationships and Calculations; Power and Effects of Electric Current

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

    "A material is a conductor because of its atomic structure, not just because it is a metal."
  ]
}

{
  "title": "Worked Example: Identifying Conductors and Insulators",
  "objective": "Demonstrate how to identify a material's electrical property by examining its atomic 'willingness' to share electrons.",
  "retrievalTextLines": [
    "Scenario: You are stripping a standard 2.5mm² Twin and Earth cable. You have the bare copper wire in one hand and the grey PVC outer sheath in the other. You need to explain why they behave differently using electron theory.",
    "Step 1: Examine the copper core. At an atomic level, copper has a valence shell (outer layer) where the electrons are far from the nucleus. This weak attraction means there are millions of 'free electrons' ready to move from atom to atom.",
    "Step 2: Examine the PVC sheath. In this material, the atoms hold onto their electrons very tightly. There are almost no 'free electrons' available to break away, meaning the electrons are 'trapped' in their orbits.",
    "Step 3: Apply the definitions. Because the copper has a high density of free electrons, it is classified as a conductor. Because the PVC lacks free electrons, it is classified as an insulator.",
    "Final Result: The copper core is the conductor used to carry the electric current, while the PVC is the insulator used to safely contain that current within the wire.",
    "Takeaway: A material's electrical property is determined by its atomic structure; specifically, whether the valence electrons are 'free' to move or 'tightly bound' to the nucleus."
  ],
  "hint": "Focus on the 'valence shell' and whether the electrons there can easily break away to become 'free electrons'."
}

parsed.title: Worked Example: Identifying Conductors and Insulators
parsed.objective: Demonstrate how to identify a material's electrical property by examining its atomic 'willingness' to share electrons.
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: You are stripping a standard 2.5mm² Twin and Earth cable. You have the bare copper wire in one hand and the grey PVC outer sheath in the other. You need to explain why they behave differently using electron theory.

Step 1: Examine the copper core. At an atomic level, copper has a valence shell (outer layer) where the electrons are far from the nucleus. This weak attraction means there are millions of 'free electrons' ready to move from atom to atom.

Step 2: Examine the PVC sheath. In this material, the atoms hold onto their electrons very tightly. There are almost no 'free electrons' available to break away, meaning the electrons are 'trapped' in their orbits.
Step 3: Apply the definitions. Because the copper has a high density of free electrons, it is classified as a conductor. Because the PVC lacks free electrons, it is classified as an insulator.
Final Result: The copper core is the conductor used to carry the electric current, while the PVC is the insulator used to safely contain that current within the wire.
Takeaway: A material's electrical property is determined by its atomic structure; specifically, whether the valence electrons are 'free' to move or 'tightly bound' to the nucleus.
parsed.hint: Focus on the 'valence shell' and whether the electrons there can easily break away to become 'free electrons'.

Worked example output:
{
  "title": "Worked Example: Identifying Conductors and Insulators",
  "objective": "Demonstrate how to identify a material's electrical property by examining its atomic 'willingness' to share electrons.",
  "retrievalTextLines": [
    "Scenario: You are stripping a standard 2.5mm² Twin and Earth cable. You have the bare copper wire in one hand and the grey PVC outer sheath in the other. You need to explain why they behave differently using electron theory.",
    "Step 1: Examine the copper core. At an atomic level, copper has a valence shell (outer layer) where the electrons are far from the nucleus. This weak attraction means there are millions of 'free electrons' ready to move from atom to atom.",
    "Step 2: Examine the PVC sheath. In this material, the atoms hold onto their electrons very tightly. There are almost no 'free electrons' available to break away, meaning the electrons are 'trapped' in their orbits.",
    "Step 3: Apply the definitions. Because the copper has a high density of free electrons, it is classified as a conductor. Because the PVC lacks free electrons, it is classified as an insulator.",
    "Final Result: The copper core is the conductor used to carry the electric current, while the PVC is the insulator used to safely contain that current within the wire.",
    "Takeaway: A material's electrical property is determined by its atomic structure; specifically, whether the valence electrons are 'free' to move or 'tightly bound' to the nucleus."
  ],
  "hint": "Focus on the 'valence shell' and whether the electrons there can easily break away to become 'free electrons'."
}

Unit: 202 - Principles of Electrical Science

Learning outcome: LO4 - Resistance, resistivity, voltage, current, power

Lesson: 202-4A - Electron Theory and Materials

Topic focus: Electron Theory and Materials

Prerequisites: 202-3B

Must-have topics: Basic electron theory; Conductors vs insulators

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 202.LO4.AC4.1: Basic electron theory
- 202.LO4.AC4.2: Conductors vs insulators

Lesson split rationale: Establishes the atomic basis of electricity and how it determines the properties of conductors and insulators.

In-scope teaching anchors: Basic electron theory; Conductors vs insulators

Out-of-scope topics: Resistance, Resistivity, and Voltage Drop; DC Circuit Relationships and Calculations; Power and Effects of Electric Current

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.

Conductors have many free electrons.
Insulators have tightly bound electrons.
integrative.questionText: Using your knowledge of atomic structure, explain why the copper core of a cable allows an electric current to flow while the PVC insulation prevents it from escaping.
answerGuidance: Copper has many free electrons in its valence shell
These electrons are loosely bound and can move easily between atoms
PVC electrons are tightly bound to their parent atoms
PVC lacks the free electrons required to facilitate a directed flow (current)
integrative.hint: Focus on the difference in how 'tightly' the atoms in each material hold onto their outer electrons.

steps.title: Worked Example: Identifying Conductors and Insulators
steps.objective: Demonstrate how to identify a material's electrical property by examining its atomic 'willingness' to share electrons.
keyIdeas: Demonstrate how to identify a material's electrical property by examining its atomic 'willingness' to share electrons.
Show one worked example.

Scenario: You are stripping a standard 2.5mm² Twin and Earth cable. You have the bare copper wire in one hand and the grey PVC outer sheath in the other. You need to explain why they behave differently using electron theory.

Step 1: Examine the copper core. At an atomic level, copper has a valence shell (outer layer) where the electrons are far from the nucleus. This weak attraction means there are millions of 'free electrons' ready to move from atom to atom.

Step 2: Examine the PVC sheath. In this material, the atoms hold onto their electrons very tightly. There are almost no 'free electrons' available to break away, meaning the electrons are 'trapped' in their orbits.
Step 3: Apply the definitions. Because the copper has a high density of free electrons, it is classified as a conductor. Because the PVC lacks free electrons, it is classified as an insulator.
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are stripping a standard 2.5mm² Twin and Earth cable. You have the bare copper wire in one hand and the grey PVC outer sheath in the other. You need to explain why they behave differently using electron theory.

Step 1: Examine the copper core. At an atomic level, copper has a valence shell (outer layer) where the electrons are far from the nucleus. This weak attraction means there are millions of 'free electrons' ready to move from atom to atom.

Step 2: Examine the PVC sheath. In this material, the atoms hold onto their electrons very tightly. There are almost no 'free electrons' available to break away, meaning the electrons are 'trapped' in their orbits.
Step 3: Apply the definitions. Because the copper has a high density of free electrons, it is classified as a conductor. Because the PVC lacks free electrons, it is classified as an insulator.
Final Result: The copper core is the conductor used to carry the electric current, while the PVC is the insulator used to safely contain that current within the wire.
taskConstraints: Strictly follow the 'teach-before-test' principle.
Do not use terms like 'Resistance' or 'Voltage' as technical definitions until they are introduced in later lessons.
Ensure all checks are answerable solely from the provided explanation content.
Do not drift into: Ohm's Law calculations
Do not drift into: Resistivity formula (rho)
Demonstrate how to identify a material's electrical property by examining its atomic 'willingness' to share electrons.

taskSkeleton.scenario: You are stripping a standard 2.5mm² Twin and Earth cable. You have the bare copper wire in one hand and the grey PVC outer sheath in the other. You need to explain why they behave differently using electron theory.
steps: Examine the copper core. At an atomic level, copper has a valence shell (outer layer) where the electrons are far from the nucleus. This weak attraction means there are millions of 'free electrons' ready to move from atom to atom.

Examine the PVC sheath. In this material, the atoms hold onto their electrons very tightly. There are almost no 'free electrons' available to break away, meaning the electrons are 'trapped' in their orbits.
Apply the definitions. Because the copper has a high density of free electrons, it is classified as a conductor. Because the PVC lacks free electrons, it is classified as an insulator.
taskSkeleton.takeaway: A material's electrical property is determined by its atomic structure; specifically, whether the valence electrons are 'free' to move or 'tightly bound' to the nucleus.

steps.hint: Focus on the 'valence shell' and whether the electrons there can easily break away to become 'free electrons'.

steps.questionText: Using your knowledge of atomic structure, explain why the copper core of a cable allows an electric current to flow while the PVC insulation prevents it from escaping.
answerGuidance: Copper has many free electrons in its valence shell
These electrons are loosely bound and can move easily between atoms
PVC electrons are tightly bound to their parent atoms
PVC lacks the free electrons required to facilitate a directed flow
steps.hint: Focus on the difference in how 'tightly' the atoms in each material hold onto their outer electrons.

spacedReview.title: Spaced Review: Atomic Basics
spacedReview.objective: Recall the charges of atomic particles and the definition of a free electron from previous theory.
retrievalTextLines: Before we get into how materials behave on site, we need to look at the 'engine room' of electricity: the atom.
You should already be familiar with the three main particles that make up an atom and how they are charged.
Remember: electricity isn't magic; it's just the movement of specific particles from one place to another.
spacedReview.questionText: Name the three particles found within an atom, state the electrical charge of each, and identify which one is capable of moving to create an electric current.
answerGuidance: The Electron (specifically the 'free electron') is the particle that moves to create current.

        "Scenario: You are stripping a standard 2.5mm² Twin and Earth cable. You have the bare copper wire in one hand and the grey PVC outer sheath in the other. You need to explain why they behave differently using electron theory.",

Unit: 202 - Principles of Electrical Science

Learning outcome: LO4 - Resistance, resistivity, voltage, current, power

Lesson: 202-4A - Electron Theory and Materials

Topic focus: Electron Theory and Materials

Prerequisites: 202-3B

Must-have topics: Basic electron theory; Conductors vs insulators

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 202.LO4.AC4.1: Basic electron theory
- 202.LO4.AC4.2: Conductors vs insulators

Lesson split rationale: Establishes the atomic basis of electricity and how it determines the properties of conductors and insulators.

In-scope teaching anchors: Basic electron theory; Conductors vs insulators

Out-of-scope topics: Resistance, Resistivity, and Voltage Drop; DC Circuit Relationships and Calculations; Power and Effects of Electric Current

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

Meaning of resistance and resistivity (in circuits) 4. Relationship between current/voltage/resistance in series & parallel D.C. circuits 5. Calculate current/voltage/resistance in series & parallel D.C. circuits 6. Calculate power in series & parallel D.C. circuits 7. Meaning of voltage drop 8. Chemical and thermal effects of electric currents ---

issues: Failed to parse refined lesson JSON.

issues.excerpt: "questionText": "What is the name of the heavy, fixed core at the center of an atom?", "answerGuidance": ["Atom"]
issues.problem: The answer guidance is factually incorrect. The core of an atom is the 'nucleus', not the 'atom' itself.
issues.whyItMatters: Learners who provide the correct technical term (nucleus) will be marked as incorrect, while those repeating the question's subject will be marked correct, reinforcing false knowledge.
issues.suggestion: Change the answer guidance for this question to 'Nucleus'.

issues.excerpt: "objective": "Classify a list of common site materials (Copper, PVC, Gold, Rubber, Steel)...", "questionText": "Using the scenario only, identify the single best wiring system or cable type."
issues.problem: The question does not align with the objective. The objective asks for classification of five materials, but the question asks for a 'wiring system' and the guidance only accepts 'PVC'.
issues.whyItMatters: The learner is never actually asked to classify the materials listed in the objective (Copper, Gold, Steel, etc.), leaving the core skill of material differentiation untested.
issues.suggestion: Rewrite the question to require the classification of the materials listed in the objective (e.g., 'Which of these are conductors and which are insulators?').

issues.excerpt: "id": "202-4a-202-4a-practice-1", "questionText": "Using the scenario only, identify the single best wiring system or cable type."
issues.problem: The Practice step is an exact duplicate of the Guided Practice step, including the misaligned question and narrow answer guidance.
issues.whyItMatters: Practice should provide independent verification of the objective. Repeating a flawed question from the previous step provides zero pedagogical value and fails to verify the learner's understanding of atomic particles or charges.
issues.suggestion: Replace this question with a set of questions covering the particles (protons, neutrons, electrons) and their charges to meet the stated objective.

issues.excerpt: "Atom is the name of the heavy, fixed core at the center of an atom."
issues.problem: Circular and incorrect definition in the anchor facts.
issues.whyItMatters: Anchor facts are meant to be the 'source of truth' for the learner. Defining the core of an atom as an 'atom' is confusing and technically wrong.
issues.suggestion: Correct the anchor fact to: 'The nucleus is the name of the heavy, fixed core at the center of an atom.'
